skill.name: active-directory-attacks
skill.description: Guides authorized security teams through Active Directory attack simulation, enumeration, credential attack paths, Kerberos abuse, relay testing, and post-compromise analysis.
skill.summary: Plan and document authorized Active Directory attack simulations and risk validation.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis findings are mostly true positives for dangerous instructional content, but false positives for hidden automatic execution because the commands are Markdown examples. The skill is high risk because it provides direct Active Directory compromise workflows, credential extraction steps, relay attacks, ticket forgery, and persistence-oriented techniques. No prompt injection attempt was found in the reviewed files.

high_findings.title: Offensive Active Directory Compromise Playbooks
high_findings.description: The skill gives direct procedures for credential harvesting, password spraying, Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, DCSync, pass-the-hash, ticket forgery, NTLM relay, and CVE exploitation. These are documentation examples, not hidden execution by the skill, but they can enable unauthorized domain compromise if misused.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static command and credential findings align with explicit offensive procedures and stated deliverables. Legitimate red-team use is possible, but the content directly enables domain compromise techniques.

high_findings.title: Credential Extraction and Privilege Escalation Guidance
high_findings.description: The advanced reference expands into DCSync-equivalent secrets dumping, SCCM and WSUS abuse, ADCS certificate impersonation, Shadow Credentials, Golden SAML, GPP password extraction, DSRM hash dumping, keytab extraction, and SSSD secret extraction. These examples target credentials, authentication material, and administrator impersonation paths.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly describe credential sources and tools for extracting or abusing authentication material. This confirms the sensitive, filesystem, and blocker findings as high-risk instructional content.

medium_findings.title: Static Command Execution Detections Are Mostly Documentation Examples
medium_findings.description: The many PowerShell, shell, cmd.exe, and tool invocation detections occur inside Markdown code blocks. The skill does not include scripts that execute these commands automatically, which lowers implementation risk but not misuse risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are clearly Markdown command examples rather than executable skill code. They still document risky operations, so the static findings should be retained as content risk evidence.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Reviewed SKILL.md and references/advanced-attacks.md for override language, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, or instructions to skip security review. No evidence found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed headers and introductory sections describe attack content but do not attempt to control the evaluator. No prompt-injection markers were found in the scanned files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Harvesting and Hash Extraction Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents commands for extracting service ticket hashes, domain controller secrets, LAPS and GMSA passwords, DSRM hashes, and key material. These examples are not executed by the skill but are dangerous operational patterns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The locations explicitly focus on obtaining hashes, passwords, certificates, and secret databases. This is a direct match for sensitive credential-access findings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Impersonation and Domain Dominance Techniques
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes Golden Ticket, Silver Ticket, constrained delegation, RBCD, ADCS impersonation, Golden SAML, and trust ticket examples. These patterns can create unauthorized administrator access when used outside an approved test.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows concrete impersonation workflows using Kerberos, delegation, certificates, and SAML. These are high-impact AD attack techniques.

content.value_statement: Active Directory attack simulations require structured steps and careful authorization. This skill organizes common AD attack paths for controlled security assessments and reporting.
actual_capabilities: Maps Active Directory reconnaissance steps using BloodHound, PowerView, and related tools.
Summarizes credential attack paths such as Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, and DCSync.
Explains Kerberos ticket abuse concepts including Golden Ticket, Silver Ticket, and pass-the-hash.
Lists NTLM relay, ADCS, SCCM, WSUS, trust, and ADFS attack scenarios.
Provides troubleshooting notes for common AD assessment failures.
Supports documentation of findings, prerequisites, and operator constraints.
limitations: It is not suitable for unapproved networks or systems.
It does not verify legal authorization or customer scope.
It does not provide defensive detection engineering by default.
It contains high-risk dual-use procedures that need expert supervision.

use_cases.title: Plan an authorized AD assessment
use_cases.description: Create a scoped checklist for domain reconnaissance, credential testing, and escalation validation.

use_cases.title: Review domain exposure paths
use_cases.description: Translate known AD weaknesses into prioritized validation steps and remediation discussion points.

use_cases.title: Prepare a red team runbook
use_cases.description: Organize tool prerequisites, attack sequence options, evidence handling, and cleanup reminders.

prompt_templates.title: Create a scoped checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an authorized Active Directory assessment checklist for a lab domain. Include prerequisites, safe validation steps, and evidence to collect.

prompt_templates.title: Map likely attack paths
prompt_templates.prompt: Given these AD observations, map likely attack paths and note the required permissions, risks, and cleanup tasks for each path.

prompt_templates.title: Compare credential risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, DCSync, and ADCS abuse for this authorized environment. Rank impact, prerequisites, and detection opportunities.

prompt_templates.title: Build an executive summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn these validated AD attack-path findings into an executive summary with business impact, evidence categories, and prioritized remediation actions.

output_examples.input: Plan a safe Active Directory attack-path review for a test domain.
output_examples.output: A phased checklist covering scope confirmation, enumeration, credential exposure review, escalation validation, evidence handling, and cleanup.

output_examples.input: Summarize the risk of Kerberoasting findings for leadership.
output_examples.output: A plain-language explanation of service account exposure, business impact, likely prerequisites, and recommended password and delegation controls.

output_examples.input: Organize ADCS misconfiguration findings into remediation actions.
output_examples.output: A prioritized remediation list covering template permissions, enrollment restrictions, certificate manager approval, monitoring, and validation retesting.

best_practices: Use only in environments where written authorization and scope are clear.
Prefer lab validation and defensive documentation before production testing.
Record credentials, tickets, created objects, and cleanup steps during every assessment.
anti_patterns: Using the procedures against networks without explicit approval.
Running credential attacks without lockout and monitoring safeguards.
Leaving forged tickets, machine accounts, certificates, or policy changes after testing.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for general users?
faq.answer: No. It contains high-risk dual-use Active Directory attack procedures for authorized security professionals.

faq.question: Does the skill execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed files are Markdown guidance and examples, not executable automation.

faq.question: Can it be used for defense?
faq.answer: Yes, defenders can use it to understand attack paths and prioritize remediation, but the content is offensive by default.

faq.question: What authorization is required?
faq.answer: Use it only with written permission, defined scope, testing windows, and agreed cleanup requirements.

faq.question: Why is publication risk high?
faq.answer: The skill includes credential extraction, relay, ticket forgery, exploitation, and persistence-oriented procedures.

faq.question: Does it include prompt injection?
faq.answer: No prompt injection attempt was found in SKILL.md or the advanced reference during this review.
